identified_error: The vitamin D level categories used in the study contained a gap: 'adequate' was defined as 75–99.75 nmol/L (30–39.9 ng/mL) and 'high-normal' was defined as ≥187.5 nmol/L (≥40 ng/mL), but 187.5 nmol/L equals 75 ng/mL, not 40 ng/mL. The correct conversion of 40 ng/mL is 100 nmol/L. This unit conversion error created a large unmapped range (99.75–187.5 nmol/L, or approximately 40–75 ng/mL) where patients would be unclassifiable, and critically misassigned patients with 25(OH)D levels in the 'high-normal' (≥40 ng/mL) reference category used in the logistic regression. This erroneous categorization of the reference group fundamentally distorted the odds ratios reported, including the headline finding that vitamin D deficiency was associated with a 14-fold increased risk of severe/critical disease relative to the ≥40 ng/mL group.
why_fatal: The incorrect unit conversion and resulting misclassification of the vitamin D categories means the reference group (≥40 ng/mL / stated as ≥187.5 nmol/L) was incorrectly defined. Since 187.5 nmol/L actually corresponds to 75 ng/mL rather than 40 ng/mL, the categorization scheme had an internal inconsistency that would misclassify patients across categories. The odds ratios, including the key OR of 14 for deficient vs. high-normal, were computed using flawed category assignments. This invalidates the primary quantitative conclusion regarding the magnitude of association between vitamin D deficiency and COVID-19 severity.
correct_approach: The authors should have used consistent and correct unit conversions throughout: 40 ng/mL = 100 nmol/L (not 187.5 nmol/L). The 'high-normal' category should have been defined as ≥100 nmol/L (≥40 ng/mL), and the 'adequate' category upper bound should have been <100 nmol/L (<40 ng/mL). With correct category boundaries, patients would have been properly classified and the regression analyses would have produced valid odds ratios for the association between vitamin D status and COVID-19 severity.
additional_errors: Selection bias: only 253 of 1176 admitted patients (21.5%) had prior vitamin D measurements, likely representing a non-random, sicker subset with more comorbidities or prior clinical suspicion of deficiency.

The 14–730 day window for historical vitamin D measurements is very wide; a measurement taken two years before infection may not reflect vitamin D status at the time of infection, even with cosinor correction.
No data on vitamin D supplementation between the historical measurement and COVID-19 infection were available, which could substantially alter actual vitamin D status at infection.
The cosinor model assumes identical seasonal amplitude for all patients, which is a strong and likely incorrect assumption.
Outlier removal in Figure 3 (values more than 2 SD from the regression fit were omitted) without sensitivity analysis is a questionable analytic choice that could bias results.
Use of one-sided p-value for BMI-vitamin D association (p=0.04 one-sided vs p=0.07 two-sided) suggests selective reporting.
